difficulty reason: It relies on bodyweight and a stability ball with minimal coordination or equipment, making it accessible to all fitness levels.

name: exercise ball lower back stretch (pyramid)
instructions:
Sit on the stability ball with your feet flat on the ground and your knees bent at a 90-degree angle.
Slowly walk your feet forward, rolling the ball down your back until your lower back is resting on the ball.
Place your hands behind your head or cross them over your chest.
Engage your core and slowly lower your upper body towards the ground, allowing your lower back to stretch over the ball.
Hold the stretch for a few seconds, then slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This ball lower back stretch targets the lats while also engaging the glutes and hamstrings, improving flexibility and spinal mobility for a healthier back.
difficulty reason: It’s a bodyweight, low‑coordination stretch that requires only a stability ball.

name: exercise ball lying side lat stretch
instructions:
Lie on your side with your legs extended and your head supported by the stability ball.
Place your top arm on the ball for stability.
Reach your top arm overhead and allow your torso to rotate slightly.
Feel the stretch in your lat muscles on the side of your body.
Hold the stretch for 20-30 seconds, then switch sides and repeat.
description: This stretch targets the latissimus dorsi and helps improve upper back flexibility while engaging the shoulders and triceps for stabilization. It increases range of motion and reduces muscle tension in the back and side torso.
difficulty reason: It uses only a stability ball and bodyweight, making it simple and low coordination.

name: exercise ball on the wall calf raise
instructions:
Stand with your back against a wall and place an exercise ball between your lower back and the wall.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart, with your toes pointing forward.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand, with your arms extended by your sides.
Raise your heels off the ground, lifting your body weight onto the balls of your feet.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This calf raise targets the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles in the calves, while also engaging the hamstrings and quadriceps for stability. It improves ankle strength, lower‑leg endurance, and balance, making it great for everyday mobility and athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It uses dumbbells and involves a compound movement that requires coordination and moderate load.

name: exercise ball on the wall calf raise (tennis ball between ankles)
instructions:
Stand facing a wall with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Place an exercise ball between the wall and your lower back.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand, with your arms extended by your sides.
Place a tennis ball between your ankles.
Raise your heels off the ground, lifting your body up onto your toes.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the calf muscles—gastrocnemius and soleus—while also engaging the hamstrings and quadriceps for stability. It strengthens the lower legs, improves ankle stability and balance, and enhances overall lower-body endurance. The added resistance from dumbbells and the instability of the tennis ball challenge coordination, making it an effective functional workout.
difficulty reason: It uses basic equipment (dumbbells) with added balance challenge, making it more demanding than a simple bodyweight calf raise but not requiring specialized gear or elite skill.

name: exercise ball on the wall calf raise (tennis ball between knees)
instructions:
Stand with your back against a wall and place an exercise ball between your lower back and the wall.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart and slightly in front of you.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand, with your arms extended by your sides.
Place a tennis ball between your knees.
Raise your heels off the ground, lifting your body up onto your toes.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the calf muscles, helping to build strength and improve ankle stability. By adding a dumbbell for resistance and a ball for balance, it also engages the quadriceps and hamstrings for a full lower‑leg workout. Regular practice can enhance power, reduce injury risk, and support athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It requires basic equipment and moderate coordination, making it suitable for those who can perform bodyweight calf raises but want to increase intensity.

name: exercise ball one leg prone lower body rotation
instructions:
Lie face down on the stability ball with your hips resting on the ball and your legs extended straight behind you.
Place your hands on the ground in front of you for support.
Engage your glutes and core muscles to stabilize your body.
Slowly lift one leg off the ground, keeping it straight and parallel to the floor.
Rotate your leg outward, away from your body, while keeping your hips and upper body stable.
Pause for a moment at the end of the rotation, then slowly return your leg to the starting position.
Repeat the rotation with the other leg.
Continue alternating legs for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This stability‑ball exercise targets the glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps, and core, helping to strengthen the posterior chain and improve hip stability. By rotating the leg while keeping the hips stable, it enhances rotational control and core engagement. It also promotes balance and functional movement patterns.
difficulty reason: It uses only bodyweight and a stability ball, with a simple movement that requires minimal coordination.

name: exercise ball one legged diagonal kick hamstring curl
instructions:
Start by lying on your back with your legs extended and your heels resting on top of the stability ball.
Place your arms by your sides for stability.
Engage your glutes and core muscles to lift your hips off the ground, creating a straight line from your shoulders to your heels.
Bend your right knee and bring it towards your chest, keeping your left leg extended and your foot flexed.
Kick your right leg diagonally across your body, extending it fully and engaging your hamstrings.
Slowly return your right leg to the starting position, maintaining control and stability.
Repeat the movement with your left leg, alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the glutes, hamstrings, and calves while engaging the core for stability. By lifting the hips and performing a diagonal kick, you strengthen the posterior chain and improve hip stability. It also enhances balance and core control through the stability ball.
difficulty reason: It requires balance on a stability ball and coordinated hip lift and leg movement, making it a complex bodyweight exercise.

name: exercise ball pike push up
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands on the floor and your shins resting on the stability ball.
Engage your core and lift your hips up towards the ceiling, rolling the ball towards your hands.
Keep your legs straight and your body in a pike position, forming an inverted V shape.
Bend your elbows and lower your upper body towards the floor, keeping your head in line with your hands.
Push through your hands and extend your arms to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The exercise targets the pectorals while engaging the shoulders, triceps, and core for stability. It improves upper body strength, shoulder mobility, and core stability through a dynamic push‑up variation on a stability ball.
difficulty reason: It uses a stability ball for balance and core engagement, making it a compound movement that requires moderate coordination and strength.

name: exercise ball prone leg raise
instructions:
Lie face down on a mat with your legs extended and your toes resting on top of the stability ball.
Place your hands on the ground, shoulder-width apart, and engage your core muscles.
Keeping your legs straight, slowly lift them off the ground, using your lower back and glutes to raise them as high as possible.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The exercise ball prone leg raise strengthens the core, glutes, and lower back, promoting spinal stability and improved posture. It also enhances core endurance and hip extension control, benefiting athletes and daily functional movements.
difficulty reason: It requires a stability ball and coordination to maintain balance while lifting the legs, making it more complex than basic bodyweight moves.

name: exercise ball seated hamstring stretch
instructions:
Sit on the stability ball with your feet flat on the ground and your knees bent at a 90-degree angle.
Slowly roll the ball forward, walking your feet out until your upper back is resting on the ball and your legs are extended straight in front of you.
Place your hands on your hips for support.
Engage your core and slowly lower your upper body towards the ground, keeping your back straight and your chest lifted.
Stop when you feel a stretch in your hamstrings, and hold the position for 20-30 seconds.
Slowly return to the starting position by pushing through your heels and using your hamstrings to pull yourself back up.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This seated hamstring stretch targets the hamstrings, glutes, and lower back, helping to improve flexibility and reduce stiffness. It also encourages core engagement and promotes better posture.
difficulty reason: It requires only a stability ball and bodyweight, with minimal coordination.

name: exercise ball seated triceps stretch
instructions:
Sit on a stability ball with your feet flat on the ground and your back straight.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand and extend your arm straight up above your head.
Bend your elbow and lower the dumbbell behind your head, keeping your upper arm close to your ear.
Hold the stretch for a few seconds, then return to the starting position.
Repeat with the other arm.
description: The seated triceps stretch on a stability ball targets the triceps and engages the shoulder muscles, improving upper arm flexibility and range of motion while reducing tension and enhancing joint mobility.
difficulty reason: It uses a stability ball and dumbbell, combining basic equipment with a controlled stretch that requires moderate coordination and balance.

name: exercise ball supine triceps extension
instructions:
Sit on an exercise ball with your feet flat on the ground and your knees bent at a 90-degree angle.
Hold a dumbbell with both hands and extend your arms straight up towards the ceiling.
Slowly lower the dumbbell behind your head, keeping your elbows close to your ears.
Pause for a moment, then raise the dumbbell back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This seated dumbbell triceps extension targets the triceps brachii, helping to strengthen the arm’s extension and improve upper‑body pushing power. The movement also engages the shoulders for stability, enhancing overall arm and shoulder endurance. Consistent practice can increase muscle definition and support functional activities that involve arm extension.
difficulty reason: It requires a dumbbell and exercise ball, combining basic equipment with a compound motion that demands moderate coordination and core stability.

name: ez bar french press on exercise ball
instructions:
Sit on an exercise ball and hold an EZ barbell with an overhand grip.
Extend your arms straight up, keeping your elbows close to your head.
Slowly lower the barbell behind your head by bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then extend your arms back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This seated EZ bar French press on an exercise ball targets the triceps while engaging the shoulders and core for stability, helping to build arm strength and improve shoulder joint control. The exercise also enhances core endurance by requiring you to maintain balance on the ball.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment (EZ bar) and requires moderate coordination and core stability.

name: ez bar lying bent arms pullover
instructions:
Lie flat on a bench with your head at one end and your feet on the floor.
Hold the EZ barbell with a pronated grip (palms facing away from you) and your hands shoulder-width apart.
Extend your arms straight above your chest, keeping a slight bend in your elbows.
Lower the barbell in an arc motion behind your head, maintaining the slight bend in your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then return the barbell to the starting position by reversing the arc motion.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ bar lying bent arms pullover targets the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the chest and triceps. It helps build upper back width and strength, improves shoulder stability and flexibility, and can contribute to a stronger, more balanced upper body.
difficulty reason: It requires an EZ barbell and a bench, and involves a compound movement that engages multiple muscle groups, making it suitable for intermediate lifters.

name: ez bar lying close grip triceps extension behind head
instructions:
Lie flat on a bench with your feet flat on the ground and your head at the end of the bench.
Hold the ez barbell with a close grip, palms facing up, and extend your arms straight up over your chest.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, slowly lower the barbell behind your head by bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then extend your arms back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ‑bar lying close‑grip triceps extension targets the long head of the triceps brachii, helping to build arm strength and improve elbow extension. It also engages the anterior deltoid and core for stability, providing a full upper‑arm workout while keeping the shoulders safe. Consistent training can enhance arm definition and support overall pushing movements.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic EZ barbell and requires moderate coordination to perform the behind‑head movement safely.

name: ez bar reverse grip bent over row
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent.
Hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, palms facing up, and hands shoulder-width apart.
Bend forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and chest up, until your torso is almost parallel to the floor.
Pull the ez barbell towards your lower chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the ez barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the upper back while also engaging the biceps and forearms, helping to improve posture and upper body pulling strength. The reverse grip variation places extra emphasis on the lower lats and brachialis for a fuller back and stronger arms.
difficulty reason: It uses an EZ bar and involves a compound bent‑over row that requires coordination and control but is accessible to most lifters.

name: ez bar seated close grip concentration curl
instructions:
Sit on a bench with your feet flat on the ground and hold an EZ barbell with an underhand grip.
Rest your elbow on the inside of your thigh, just above the knee.
Curl the barbell up towards your shoulder while keeping your upper arm stationary.
Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement, then slowly lower the barbell back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This seated close‑grip concentration curl isolates the biceps brachii and the brachialis, strengthening the upper arm and improving forearm endurance. The controlled movement enhances peak contraction and promotes muscle growth while minimizing shoulder involvement.
difficulty reason: It requires only a seated position and a barbell, making it easy for beginners to perform with proper form.

name: ez bar standing french press
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an overhand grip.
Raise the barbell above your head, fully extending your arms.
Keeping your upper arms close to your head, slowly lower the barbell behind your head by bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then extend your arms back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ‑bar standing French press isolates the triceps brachii while also engaging the shoulders for stability and control. By extending the elbows overhead, it improves arm strength, joint mobility, and can help reduce shoulder strain compared to a straight bar. Regularly incorporating this move can enhance upper‑arm definition and support daily lifting tasks.
difficulty reason: It requires a basic piece of equipment (EZ barbell) and involves a compound overhead movement that demands moderate coordination and strength.

name: ez barbell anti gravity press
instructions:
Start by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart and holding the ez barbell with an overhand grip.
Raise the barbell to shoulder height, keeping your elbows slightly bent and your palms facing forward.
Press the barbell overhead, extending your arms fully.
Lower the barbell back to shoulder height and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell anti gravity press works the deltoids, triceps, and upper chest, helping to build shoulder strength and stability. It engages the core and lower body for balance, improving overall upper‑body power. By pressing the bar overhead, it also enhances shoulder mobility and posture.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment and a compound movement that requires coordination but is not overly complex.

name: ez barbell close grip preacher curl
instructions:
Sit on a preacher curl bench and place your upper arms on the pad, gripping the ez barbell with an underhand grip.
Rest your triceps on the pad and fully extend your arms, keeping your back straight.
Slowly curl the barbell towards your shoulders, contracting your biceps.
Pause for a moment at the top, squeezing your biceps.
Lower the barbell back to the starting position, fully extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell close‑grip preacher curl targets the biceps brachii and engages the forearm flexors, helping to build peak contraction and arm definition. By isolating the biceps, it reduces strain on the shoulders and improves overall arm strength and aesthetics.
difficulty reason: It requires an EZ barbell and a preacher bench, making it a moderate‑equipment exercise that is more advanced than bodyweight but less complex than heavy compound lifts.

name: ez barbell close-grip curl
instructions:
Stand up straight with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and your upper arms stationary throughout the movement.
Curl the barbell up towards your shoulders, contracting your biceps.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell close‑grip curl targets the biceps brachii and also engages the forearm flexors. It helps build arm strength, improve grip endurance, and enhance the appearance of the upper arms. The close grip reduces shoulder involvement, making it a safe, isolated move for beginners.
difficulty reason: It is a simple, low‑coordination isolation movement that can be performed with an EZ barbell, making it suitable for beginners.

name: ez barbell curl
instructions:
Stand up straight with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, palms facing up.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and your upper arms stationary throughout the movement.
Exhale as you curl the barbell up towards your shoulders, contracting your biceps.
Pause for a moment at the top, then inhale as you slowly lower the barbell back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell curl targets the biceps brachii and brachialis in the upper arms, while also engaging forearm flexors. It helps build arm strength, improve muscle definition, and enhance grip stability. This exercise is effective for overall upper arm development and functional lifting.
difficulty reason: It uses a simple, low‑coordination movement that can be performed with basic equipment.

name: ez barbell decline close grip face press
instructions:
Lie on a decline bench with your head lower than your feet.
Grasp the ez barbell with a close grip, palms facing each other.
Extend your arms straight up above your chest, keeping your elbows close to your body.
Lower the barbell towards your forehead by bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then press the barbell back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell decline close‑grip face press targets the triceps brachii, engaging the long and lateral heads while also recruiting the chest and anterior shoulder for stability. This movement improves upper‑arm strength, increases elbow extension power, and enhances shoulder joint stability during pressing motions.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment (EZ barbell) and a compound movement that requires moderate coordination and strength, fitting the intermediate level.

name: ez barbell decline triceps extension
instructions:
Lie on a decline bench with your head lower than your feet and your feet secured.
Hold the ez barbell with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Extend your arms fully, keeping your elbows close to your head.
Lower the barbell slowly towards your forehead, bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then extend your arms back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Targets the triceps brachii, especially the long head, with a decline angle that emphasizes the upper portion. This exercise builds arm strength, size, and improves pushing power.
difficulty reason: It uses an EZ barbell on a decline bench, requiring moderate coordination and equipment, fitting the intermediate level.

name: ez barbell incline triceps extension
instructions:
Set up an incline bench at a 45-degree angle.
Sit on the bench with your back against the pad and hold the ez barbell with an overhand grip.
Extend your arms fully overhead, keeping your elbows close to your head.
Lower the barbell behind your head by bending your elbows, keeping your upper arms stationary.
Pause for a moment, then extend your arms back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell incline triceps extension targets the triceps brachii, helping to build arm strength and size while also engaging the anterior deltoids for shoulder stability. It improves the lock‑out power of the elbow joint and enhances overall upper‑body aesthetics.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment and requires controlled movement, making it suitable for those with some experience but not yet advanced.

name: ez barbell jm bench press
instructions:
Lie flat on a bench with your feet flat on the ground and your back pressed against the bench.
Grasp the ez barbell with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Lower the barbell to your chest, keeping your elbows tucked in close to your body.
Push the barbell back up to the starting position, fully extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This EZ barbell JM bench press works the triceps, chest, and shoulders, helping to build upper‑body strength, improve pushing power, and enhance muscle symmetry. By keeping elbows tucked and using a slightly wider grip, it emphasizes triceps activation while also engaging the pectoral and deltoid muscles for a balanced upper‑body workout.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment (an EZ barbell) and involves a compound movement that targets multiple upper‑body muscle groups.

name: ez barbell reverse grip curl
instructions:
Stand up straight with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and your upper arms stationary throughout the exercise.
Curl the barbell upwards by contracting your biceps, while exhaling.
Continue to raise the barbell until your biceps are fully contracted and the barbell is at shoulder level.
Hold the contracted position for a brief pause as you squeeze your biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the barbell back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ bar reverse grip curl primarily works the biceps brachii and brachialis while also engaging the forearm flexors. It improves arm strength, increases muscle size, and enhances grip endurance, making it a versatile addition to upper‑arm training.
difficulty reason: Because it requires an EZ barbell and involves a controlled, compound curling motion that is more complex than bodyweight moves but still accessible to most lifters.

name: ez barbell reverse grip preacher curl
instructions:
Sit on a preacher bench with your chest against the pad and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the ez barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Rest your upper arms on the pad, allowing your forearms to hang down.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, exhale and curl the barbell up towards your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, then inhale and slowly lower the barbell back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell reverse grip preacher curl targets the biceps brachii and brachialis, while also engaging the forearm flexors. This isolation movement improves peak biceps contraction and can help develop a fuller arm appearance. It also reduces elbow joint stress compared to a standard curl.
difficulty reason: The exercise uses a specialized bench and requires controlled, isolated movement, making it appropriate for intermediate lifters.

name: ez barbell seated curls
instructions:
Sit on a bench with your feet flat on the ground and your back straight.
Hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, palms facing up, and your hands shoulder-width apart.
Rest your upper arms on your thighs, allowing the ez barbell to hang down in front of you.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, exhale and curl the ez barbell upwards towards your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, squeezing your biceps.
Inhale and slowly lower the ez barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This seated EZ barbell curl targets the biceps brachii and brachialis, strengthening the upper arm and improving forearm endurance. It also helps improve grip strength and overall arm aesthetics.
difficulty reason: It requires a basic piece of equipment and a simple, controlled movement that is suitable for most lifters.

name: ez barbell seated triceps extension
instructions:
Sit on a bench with your back straight and feet flat on the ground.
Hold the ez barbell with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Raise the barbell overhead, fully extending your arms.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, lower the barbell behind your head by bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then raise the barbell back to the starting position by extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell seated triceps extension targets the triceps brachii, helping to build strength and definition in the upper arms while also engaging the shoulder stabilizers. This exercise improves elbow extension power and contributes to better performance in pressing movements. It is a focused, isolation movement that can be incorporated into both strength and hypertrophy training.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment and requires moderate coordination to perform the movement correctly.

name: ez barbell spider curl
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, palms facing up.
Rest your upper arms on a preacher bench or stability ball, allowing your elbows to hang down.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, exhale and curl the barbell up towards your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, squeezing your biceps.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell spider curl targets the biceps brachii and engages the forearms, helping to build arm strength and improve peak contraction. By isolating the biceps in a controlled motion, it reduces shoulder involvement and enhances muscle definition. Consistent practice can increase arm size and support overall upper‑body strength.
difficulty reason: It uses basic equipment (EZ barbell) and focuses on a compound‑style isolation movement that requires moderate coordination.

name: ez barbell spider curl
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, palms facing up.
Rest your upper arms on a preacher bench or stability ball, allowing your elbows to hang down.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, exhale and curl the barbell up towards your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, squeezing your biceps.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ barbell spider curl targets the biceps brachii while engaging the forearms for a focused upper arm workout. By isolating the biceps with the upper arms stationary, it enhances peak contraction and improves muscle definition. Consistent practice can increase arm strength and support overall upper body stability.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic EZ barbell and requires controlled movement, making it suitable for those with moderate experience.

name: ez-bar biceps curl (with arm blaster)
instructions:
Stand up straight with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, palms facing up.
Place your upper arms against the arm blaster, keeping them stationary throughout the exercise.
Keeping your elbows close to your body, exhale and curl the barbell up towards your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, squeezing your biceps.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position, fully extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the biceps brachii and also engages the forearm flexors. By keeping the upper arms stationary on the arm blaster, it isolates the biceps, allowing for a focused contraction and reduced strain on the elbows. Consistent performance improves arm strength, size, and functional pulling power.
difficulty reason: It requires basic equipment (EZ barbell and arm blaster) and focuses on a single-joint movement, making it suitable for intermediate lifters.

name: ez-bar close-grip bench press
instructions:
Lie flat on a bench with your feet flat on the ground and your back pressed against the bench.
Grasp the ez barbell with a close grip, hands shoulder-width apart, palms facing forward.
Lift the barbell off the rack and hold it directly above your chest with your arms fully extended.
Slowly lower the barbell towards your chest, keeping your elbows close to your body.
Pause for a moment when the barbell touches your chest.
Push the barbell back up to the starting position, fully extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The ez‑bar close‑grip bench press targets the triceps while also engaging the chest and anterior deltoids. It builds upper‑body strength and improves elbow stability, making it ideal for both hypertrophy and functional performance.
difficulty reason: It is a compound exercise performed with a basic ez barbell, requiring moderate strength and coordination.

name: ez-barbell standing wide grip biceps curl
instructions:
Stand up straight with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the ez barbell with an underhand grip, hands wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and your upper arms stationary throughout the movement.
Curl the barbell up towards your shoulders by contracting your biceps.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The EZ‑bar standing wide‑grip biceps curl focuses on the biceps brachii while also engaging the forearms, helping to build upper‑arm strength and size. The movement improves elbow flexion and shoulder stability, making it useful for everyday lifting and sports performance.
difficulty reason: It uses an EZ barbell and requires controlled movement, making it suitable for those with basic equipment experience but not advanced skill.

name: farmers walk
instructions:
Stand up straight with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing your sides.
Keep your back straight and your shoulders back.
Take small, controlled steps forward, maintaining an upright posture.
Continue walking for the desired distance or time.
To finish, stop walking and carefully lower the dumbbells to your sides.
description: The farmer’s walk engages the quads, calves, forearms, and core, building strength, stability, and grip endurance while also improving posture and functional mobility. It’s a full‑body conditioning exercise that boosts power and muscular endurance. The movement also enhances core stability and improves overall athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It requires basic equipment (dumbbells) and involves compound, coordinated movement, fitting the intermediate level.

name: finger curls
instructions:
Sit on a bench with your feet flat on the ground and hold a barbell with an underhand grip, palms facing up.
Rest your forearms on your thighs, allowing your wrists to hang off the edge.
Slowly curl your fingers towards your palms, squeezing the barbell tightly.
Hold the contraction for a moment, then slowly release your fingers back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Finger curls with a barbell target the wrist flexors and grip muscles of the forearms, helping to strengthen the forearm extensors and improve overall grip endurance. By isolating the wrist flexion, this exercise builds muscular endurance and enhances hand strength for daily tasks and sports that require a strong grip.
difficulty reason: The movement is simple, requires minimal coordination, and can be performed safely with a barbell for beginners.

name: flag
instructions:
Start by gripping a vertical pole with both hands, palms facing each other, and arms fully extended.
Engage your core and lift your legs off the ground, keeping them straight.
Using your core and upper body strength, raise your legs until they are parallel to the ground.
Hold this position for as long as you can, maintaining a straight body line.
Slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This hanging leg raise targets the obliques, rectus abdominis, hip flexors, and shoulders, engaging the core and upper body simultaneously. It builds core strength, improves shoulder stability, and enhances trunk control for better posture. Consistent practice also increases grip strength and overall functional fitness.
difficulty reason: The exercise requires body‑weight core stability, grip strength, and coordination, fitting the intermediate level.

name: flexion leg sit up (bent knee)
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engaging your abs, slowly lift your upper body off the ground, curling forward until your torso is at a 45-degree angle.
At the same time, lift your legs off the ground, bending your knees and bringing them towards your chest.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body and legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the rectus abdominis and hip flexors, strengthening core stability and improving trunk control. It also helps develop lumbar flexibility and can aid in better posture during daily activities.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight movement with straightforward mechanics and minimal coordination requirements.

name: flexion leg sit up (straight arm)
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your legs extended and arms straight above your head.
Engaging your abs, lift your upper body off the ground while simultaneously lifting your legs towards your chest.
Reach your hands towards your toes as you lift your upper body and legs.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body and legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the abdominal muscles while also engaging the hip flexors and quadriceps. It improves core stability, hip flexion strength, and overall trunk control, making it useful for functional movement and athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It’s a body‑weight compound movement that requires moderate core strength and coordination.

name: floor fly (with barbell)
instructions:
Lie flat on your back on the floor with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Hold a barbell with an overhand grip, arms extended straight up over your chest.
Slowly lower the barbell out to the sides, keeping a slight bend in your elbows.
Lower the barbell until your arms are parallel to the floor, feeling a stretch in your chest.
Pause for a moment, then squeeze your chest muscles to bring the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The floor fly with barbell targets the pectoralis major while engaging the deltoids and triceps as stabilizers. It helps build chest strength and shape while improving shoulder flexibility and muscle endurance. This exercise also reduces lower back strain compared to a flat bench fly.
difficulty reason: It requires a barbell and some coordination, but is still a straightforward isolation movement.

name: flutter kicks
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your legs extended and your hands by your sides.
Engage your core and lift your legs off the ground about 6 inches.
Keeping your legs straight, alternate lifting one leg slightly higher than the other.
Continue this fluttering motion for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Flutter kicks target the hip flexors and lower abs while also engaging the glutes for stability. This exercise improves core strength, hip mobility, and endurance, making it ideal for a full lower‑body and core workout.
difficulty reason: It is a body‑weight move that relies on simple, alternating leg motions, making it accessible and easy to perform for beginners.

name: forward jump
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Bend your knees and lower your body into a squat position.
Swing your arms back for momentum.
Jump forward explosively, extending your hips, knees, and ankles.
Land softly on the balls of your feet and immediately go into the next jump.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This plyometric drill targets the quads, calves, hamstrings, and glutes, boosting explosive power, lower‑leg strength, and coordination.
difficulty reason: It involves bodyweight plyometrics that demand more coordination and power than basic bodyweight movements.

name: forward lunge (male)
instructions:
Stand with your feet hip-width apart and hands on your hips.
Take a big step forward with your right foot, lowering your body into a lunge position.
Bend your right knee to about 90 degrees, keeping your knee aligned with your ankle.
Push off with your right foot and return to the starting position.
Repeat with your left leg, alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The forward lunge targets the glutes, quadriceps, hamstrings, and calves, strengthening the lower body while improving balance and stability. It also helps to increase hip flexibility and core engagement, making it ideal for overall leg development.
difficulty reason: Because it uses only bodyweight and requires minimal coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: frankenstein squat
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold the barbell in front of your body with straight arms.
Keeping your back straight, lower your body by bending at the knees and hips, as if sitting back into a chair.
Continue lowering until your thighs are parallel to the ground, or as low as you can comfortably go.
Pause for a moment, then push through your heels to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The Frankenstein squat works the glutes, quadriceps, hamstrings, and calves, improving lower‑body strength, hip stability, and overall functional movement. It also enhances core control and posture by requiring a stable, upright torso throughout the lift.
difficulty reason: It uses a barbell for resistance and involves a compound squat movement, fitting the intermediate level.

name: frog crunch
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engaging your abs, lift your upper body off the ground, curling forward until your torso is at a 45-degree angle.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The frog crunch targets the abdominal muscles and hip flexors, engaging the core to improve stability and torso control. It helps strengthen the lower abs, improve posture, and enhance core endurance.
difficulty reason: It requires only body weight and basic coordination, making it accessible for beginners.

name: frog planche
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands shoulder-width apart and your feet together.
Bend your elbows and lower your body towards the ground, keeping your back straight.
As you lower your body, lift your feet off the ground and bring your knees towards your chest.
Hold this position for a few seconds, then extend your legs back out and push yourself back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The frog planche targets the core, especially the abs, while also engaging the shoulders, chest, and triceps for stabilization. This exercise improves core strength, balance, and overall upper-body stability, helping to build a strong foundation for more advanced gymnastics moves. It also enhances coordination and body awareness by keeping the hips raised and knees tucked.
difficulty reason: It is a body‑weight exercise that demands core stability and coordination, fitting the intermediate level of complexity.

name: front lever
instructions:
Start by hanging from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Engage your core and pull your shoulder blades down and back.
Bend your knees and tuck them towards your chest.
Simultaneously, lift your legs up and extend them straight out in front of you, keeping your body parallel to the ground.
Hold this position for as long as you can, aiming for a full front lever position.
To release, slowly lower your legs back down and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The front lever engages the core, lats, shoulders, and forearms to create a rigid, horizontal body position. It develops explosive upper‑body strength, core stability, and advanced body‑control skills, making it a powerful functional exercise for athletes and calisthenics enthusiasts. Consistent practice can also improve posture and reduce the risk of shoulder injuries by strengthening stabilizing muscles.
difficulty reason: It demands exceptional core and upper‑body strength, precise body control, and a high skill level that only experienced practitioners can achieve.

name: front lever reps
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and pull your shoulder blades down and back.
Keeping your body straight, lift your legs up until they are parallel to the ground.
Hold this position for as long as you can, aiming for 10-20 seconds.
Slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The front lever is a bodyweight exercise that targets the upper back, core, and shoulders, engaging the latissimus dorsi, rhomboids, and abdominal muscles. It builds isometric strength, improves core stability, and enhances overall upper-body control.
difficulty reason: It requires maximal core and upper-body strength and isometric control, placing it in the expert category.

name: front plank with twist
instructions:
Start in a high plank position with your hands directly under your shoulders and your body in a straight line from head to toe.
Engage your core and glutes to maintain a stable position.
Rotate your torso to the right, lifting your right arm and extending it towards the ceiling.
Keep your hips and legs stable as you twist.
Hold for a moment, then return to the starting position.
Repeat the twist on the left side.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The front plank with twist targets the abdominal core, especially the obliques, while engaging the shoulders and glutes for stability. It improves core strength, rotational control, and overall torso endurance, helping to build a solid foundation for functional movement and injury prevention.
difficulty reason: The twist adds coordination and rotational demand beyond a basic plank, requiring active core engagement and stability.

name: full maltese
instructions:
Start by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart and your arms extended straight out to the sides.
Slowly lean forward, keeping your arms straight, until your upper body is parallel to the ground.
Engage your core and hold this position for a few seconds.
Return to the starting position by pushing through your feet and standing back up.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This standing bodyweight exercise works the core, particularly the obliques and transverse abdominis, while also engaging the shoulders and chest for stability. It improves posture, core strength, and balance, making it ideal for beginners looking to build foundational strength.
difficulty reason: It requires only body weight and basic coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: full planche
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands shoulder-width apart and your fingers pointing forward.
Engage your core and slowly shift your weight forward, lifting your feet off the ground.
Continue shifting your weight forward until your body is parallel to the ground, balancing on your hands.
Hold this position for as long as you can, maintaining a straight body line.
Slowly lower your feet back to the ground and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The full planche is a body‑weight exercise that targets the core, especially the abs, while simultaneously engaging the shoulders, chest, and triceps for stability and strength. It improves dynamic balance, core control, and upper‑body power, making it ideal for advanced calisthenics progression.
difficulty reason: It requires maximal body‑weight control, advanced shoulder and core strength, and precise coordination.

name: full planche push-up
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands placed slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Engage your core and lower your body down towards the ground, keeping your elbows close to your sides.
As you lower yourself, lean your body forward and lift your feet off the ground, balancing on your hands.
Continue to lower your body until your chest is just above the ground.
Push through your hands and extend your arms to lift your body back up to the starting position.
Maintain a straight line from your head to your heels throughout the movement.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The full planche push‑up targets the pectorals while engaging the shoulders, triceps, and core for stability and power. It builds upper‑body pushing strength, improves shoulder stability, and enhances core control and body balance, making it a key progression toward a full planche.
difficulty reason: It requires elite body‑weight control, significant strength, and advanced coordination beyond typical compound movements.

name: gironda sternum chin
instructions:
Stand facing a high bar with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Reach up and grab the bar with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hang from the bar with your arms fully extended and your body straight.
Engage your lats and biceps to pull your chest up towards the bar, leading with your sternum.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower yourself back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This bodyweight pull‑up variation targets the latissimus dorsi, biceps, rhomboids, and rear deltoids, engaging the upper back and arms in a compound motion. It improves upper body strength, posture, and grip endurance while also enhancing shoulder stability.
difficulty reason: It requires a moderate level of upper body strength and coordination, making it suitable for intermediate trainees.

name: glute bridge march
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Engage your glutes and lift your hips off the ground, forming a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
While keeping your hips lifted, lift one foot off the ground and bring your knee towards your chest.
Lower your foot back to the ground and repeat the movement with the other leg.
Continue alternating legs in a marching motion while maintaining the bridge position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The glute bridge march targets the glutes while also engaging the hamstrings and quadriceps, helping to strengthen the posterior chain and improve hip stability. It also enhances core stability and promotes balanced lower‑leg development. This body‑weight exercise can be performed anywhere and is great for building strength without equipment.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight, requires basic coordination, and is a simple variation of the glute bridge.

name: glute bridge two legs on bench (male)
instructions:
Sit on the edge of a bench with your back against it and your feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands on the bench beside your hips for support.
Engage your glutes and hamstrings, then lift your hips off the bench until your body forms a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your hips back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the glutes while also engaging the hamstrings and core for stability. It improves hip extension strength and posterior chain activation, helping to enhance athletic performance and posture.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight on a bench, requiring minimal coordination and equipment, making it suitable for beginners.

name: glute-ham raise
instructions:
Adjust the glute-ham raise machine to fit your body.
Position yourself face down on the machine with your ankles secured.
Place your hands on your chest or cross them over your chest.
Engage your hamstrings and glutes to lift your upper body up towards the ceiling.
Continue lifting until your body is in a straight line from your head to your heels.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The glute-ham raise targets the hamstrings, glutes, and lower back, helping build posterior chain strength and improve hip extension and core stability. This exercise also enhances functional movement patterns and can aid in injury prevention for athletes.
difficulty reason: It requires specialized equipment and coordination to stabilize the body while lifting and lowering, making it suitable for advanced trainees.

name: gorilla chin
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and your knees slightly bent.
Grasp a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hang from the bar with your arms fully extended and your palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and pull your body up towards the bar, bringing your chin above the bar.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The gorilla chin‑up targets the upper back, biceps, and forearms while engaging the core to stabilize the waist. It improves upper‑body pulling strength, shoulder stability, and core endurance, helping build a stronger, more defined midsection. The exercise also enhances grip strength and overall functional fitness.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight compound pulling movement that requires coordination and upper‑body strength, fitting the intermediate category.

name: groin crunch
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engaging your abs, lift your legs off the ground, bringing your knees towards your chest.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The groin crunch targets the hip flexors and inner thighs while engaging the core, helping to strengthen the abdominal muscles and improve hip stability. It also enhances core endurance and aids in developing a well-balanced lower body. Performing this exercise can improve posture, reduce lower back strain, and support athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight move that requires minimal coordination and equipment.

name: hack calf raise
instructions:
Adjust the sled machine to a comfortable weight.
Stand on the sled machine with your toes on the platform and your heels hanging off.
Hold onto the handles for stability.
Raise your heels as high as possible by pushing through the balls of your feet.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The sled hack calf raise targets the calves, especially the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, while also engaging the hamstrings and glutes for stability. This exercise strengthens the lower legs, improves ankle power and balance, and can enhance performance in sprinting and jumping. By using a sled, you can add progressive resistance and reduce joint stress compared to bodyweight calf raises.
difficulty reason: It uses a sled machine, which is a piece of equipment that requires moderate coordination and resistance training experience.

name: hack one leg calf raise
instructions:
Adjust the sled machine to an appropriate weight.
Stand on the sled machine with one foot, keeping the other foot off the ground.
Hold onto the handles for stability.
Raise your heel as high as possible, lifting your body up on the ball of your foot.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heel back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
Switch legs and repeat the exercise.
description: The hack one‑leg calf raise targets the calf muscles while also engaging the hamstrings and glutes for stability, improving ankle strength, balance, and lower‑leg power. It helps increase calf hypertrophy and enhances functional movement for sports that require explosive pushes. This exercise also develops single‑leg coordination and core stability.
difficulty reason: It requires specialized sled equipment and complex single‑leg coordination, making it suitable for advanced lifters.

name: half knee bends (male)
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Bend your knees and lower your body down as if you were sitting back into a chair.
Keep your chest up and your weight in your heels.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push through your heels to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Half knee bends target the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes, while also engaging the core for stability. This movement improves lower‑body strength and helps elevate heart rate for cardiovascular conditioning. It’s a low‑impact exercise suitable for all fitness levels.
difficulty reason: It requires only body weight, simple form, and minimal coordination, making it ideal for those starting their fitness journey.

name: half sit-up (male)
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engaging your abs, slowly lift your upper body off the ground, curling forward until your torso is at a 45-degree angle.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Half sit-ups target the abdominal muscles, especially the rectus abdominis and hip flexors, helping to strengthen core stability and improve posture. This exercise also enhances lumbar flexibility and can aid in reducing lower back strain.
difficulty reason: It is a simple bodyweight movement that requires minimal coordination and equipment.

name: hamstring stretch
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Step forward with your right foot and shift your weight onto your right leg.
Keeping your back straight, slowly bend forward at the hips, reaching towards your right foot with both hands.
Hold the stretch for 20-30 seconds, then return to the starting position.
Repeat on the other side.
description: This hamstring stretch targets the hamstrings and glutes, helping to increase lower‑leg flexibility and relieve tightness that can cause lower‑back discomfort. It also improves posture by elongating the posterior chain. By holding the stretch, you promote better range of motion and reduce injury risk during daily activities.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight with straightforward forward‑bending movements that require minimal coordination.

name: hands bike
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and handlebar position to a comfortable level.
Sit on the ergometer with your back straight and feet on the pedals.
Grasp the handles with your hands and position your arms at a 90-degree angle.
Start pedaling with your hands, pushing and pulling the handles in a controlled motion.
Continue pedaling for the desired duration or number of repetitions.
description: The hands bike on an upper body ergometer targets the pectorals, triceps, and shoulders, helping to build upper‑body strength and endurance while improving cardiovascular fitness. By engaging these muscle groups in a controlled push‑pull motion, it also enhances core stability and posture.
difficulty reason: It requires basic equipment and involves a compound upper‑body movement that is more complex than simple bodyweight exercises.

name: hands clasped circular toe touch (male)
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and your hands clasped together in front of your chest.
Bend your knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips, keeping your back straight.
Lower your hands towards your toes in a circular motion, reaching as far as you can without straining.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The hands clasped circular toe touch is a bodyweight stretch that targets the glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps, and calves. It improves hip flexibility, strengthens the posterior chain, and enhances lower‑leg circulation, while promoting core stability through controlled hip hinge movement.
difficulty reason: It requires only bodyweight and basic coordination, making it accessible for beginners.

name: hands reversed clasped circular toe touch (male)
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and your arms extended to the sides.
Bend forward at the waist, keeping your back straight and your knees slightly bent.
Reach down with your hands and clasp them together behind your legs.
Slowly raise your hands up and over your head in a circular motion, keeping your legs straight.
Continue the circular motion until your hands touch your toes.
Reverse the motion and bring your hands back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This bodyweight movement targets the glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps, and calves, helping to strengthen the lower body while improving flexibility and posture. By bending forward and extending the legs in a controlled circular motion, you engage the posterior chain and promote core stability. The exercise also enhances balance and joint mobility.
difficulty reason: It requires only bodyweight and a simple forward bend, making it accessible with minimal coordination or equipment.

name: handstand
instructions:
Find an open space with enough room to perform a handstand.
Place your hands on the ground shoulder-width apart, fingers pointing forward.
Kick your legs up towards the wall, using your core and shoulders to maintain balance.
Once in a handstand position, engage your triceps to support your body weight.
Hold the handstand for as long as you can maintain balance.
To come down, slowly lower your legs back to the ground.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The handstand primarily targets the triceps, shoulders, and core. It builds upper body strength, improves balance, and enhances core stability. This bodyweight exercise also promotes joint stability and functional strength.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight compound movement that engages multiple muscle groups and requires coordination and balance.

name: handstand push-up
instructions:
Find a wall and face away from it, standing a few feet away.
Place your hands on the ground shoulder-width apart and kick your feet up against the wall, coming into a handstand position.
Bend your elbows and lower your head towards the ground, keeping your body in a straight line.
Push through your hands and extend your arms to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Handstand push‑ups target the triceps, shoulders, chest, and core, building explosive upper‑body strength while enhancing balance and core stability. They improve shoulder stability, increase upper‑body power, and train body control against gravity.
difficulty reason: The exercise requires advanced balance, shoulder stability, and core control beyond basic body‑weight moves.

name: hanging leg hip raise
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with your arms fully extended and your palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and lift your legs up by flexing your hips and knees until your thighs are parallel to the ground.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The hanging leg hip raise targets the abs, hip flexors, and lower back, strengthening core stability and improving posture. It also enhances hip flexibility and builds endurance in the abdominal and lower back muscles. Consistent practice can lead to a stronger, more defined core and better overall functional movement.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight exercise that primarily targets core muscles with minimal equipment and coordination demands.

name: hanging leg raise
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with your arms fully extended and your palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and lift your legs up in front of you, keeping them straight.
Continue lifting until your legs are parallel to the ground or as high as you can comfortably go.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The hanging leg raise targets the abdominal muscles, especially the rectus abdominis and hip flexors, strengthening the core and improving trunk stability. It also engages the lower back and shoulders for support, helping to build overall core endurance and improve posture.
difficulty reason: It requires bodyweight control and coordination, fitting the intermediate level of difficulty.

name: hanging oblique knee raise
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with your arms fully extended and your palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and lift your knees towards your chest, twisting your torso to the side as you do so.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat on the other side, twisting your torso in the opposite direction.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the oblique abdominal muscles, engaging the core and improving rotational strength and stability. It also helps to strengthen the hip flexors and improve overall core endurance.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight and a pull-up bar, making it suitable for beginners.

name: hanging pike
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with your arms fully extended and your palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and lift your legs up towards the bar, keeping them straight.
Continue lifting until your body forms a 'V' shape, with your legs parallel to the ground.
Hold the position for a moment, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The hanging pike targets the abdominal core, hip flexors, and shoulders, engaging the entire upper body and core simultaneously. It improves core stability, shoulder mobility, and hip flexor strength, while also enhancing grip endurance. This exercise helps develop a stronger, more defined midsection and better posture.
difficulty reason: It requires a solid core and grip strength to maintain the hanging position and lift the legs, making it more demanding than basic bodyweight moves but still achievable without specialized equipment.

name: hanging straight leg hip raise
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with your arms fully extended and your palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and lift your legs up in front of you until they are parallel to the ground.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the abdominal muscles and hip flexors, strengthening the core and improving hip flexion control. It helps build lower abdominal endurance, enhances posture, and supports functional movements that involve trunk stability.
difficulty reason: It requires a pull‑up bar, core stability, and coordination, making it suitable for intermediate‑level trainees.

name: hanging straight leg raise
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with your arms fully extended and your palms facing away from you.
Engage your core and lift your legs up in front of you, keeping them straight.
Continue lifting until your legs are parallel to the ground or as high as you can comfortably go.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the abdominal muscles and hip flexors, strengthening the core and improving hip stability. It helps build explosive power, enhances posture, and supports lower back health.
difficulty reason: It requires grip strength and core control, fitting an intermediate level.

name: hanging straight twisting leg hip raise
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with your arms fully extended and your legs straight.
Engage your core and lift your legs up towards your chest, keeping them straight.
Once your legs are parallel to the ground, twist your hips to one side, bringing your legs towards that side.
Pause for a moment, then return to the starting position.
Repeat the movement, but this time twist your hips to the opposite side.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The hanging straight twisting leg hip raise targets the obliques and hip flexors while engaging the entire core for stability. This exercise improves core strength, rotational control, and hip flexor flexibility, helping to strengthen the waist and enhance athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It requires a pull-up bar and coordinated core activation, making it suitable for intermediate trainees.

name: high knee against wall
instructions:
Stand facing a wall with your feet hip-width apart.
Place your hands on the wall for support.
Engage your core and lift your right knee up towards your chest, while keeping your left foot on the ground.
Quickly switch legs, bringing your left knee up towards your chest and lowering your right foot back down.
Continue alternating legs in a running motion, bringing your knees up as high as possible.
Maintain a fast pace and keep your upper body stable throughout the exercise.
Repeat for the desired duration or number of repetitions.
description: High knees against the wall target the quadriceps, hamstrings, glutes, and calves while providing a vigorous cardiovascular workout. This exercise increases heart rate, improves lower‑body strength, and enhances running mechanics by emphasizing knee lift and core stability.
difficulty reason: It is a body‑weight movement that is simple to perform and requires minimal coordination.

name: hip raise (bent knee)
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands by your sides, palms facing down.
Engage your core and glutes, then lift your hips off the ground until your body forms a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your hips back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The bent‑knee hip raise targets the glutes, hamstrings, and core, helping strengthen the posterior chain and improve hip stability. It also engages the abs for stability, providing a low‑impact way to build hip lift and core endurance.
difficulty reason: It is a basic bodyweight movement that requires minimal coordination and no equipment.

name: hug keens to chest
instructions:
Start by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Bend your knees and lower your body down into a squat position.
As you squat down, bring your knees up towards your chest and hug them with your arms.
Hold this position for a moment, then slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise primarily works the glutes, hamstrings, and quadriceps, improving lower body strength and stability. It also engages core muscles for balance and helps increase hip mobility.
difficulty reason: It relies only on body weight and involves a straightforward squat motion with minimal coordination.

name: hyght dumbbell fly
instructions:
Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing each other.
Extend your arms straight up over your chest, with a slight bend in your elbows.
Keeping a slight bend in your elbows, lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch in your chest.
Pause for a moment, then reverse the movement and bring the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The high dumbbell fly targets the pectoral muscles, especially the upper chest, while also engaging the anterior deltoids and triceps for stabilization. This exercise helps to improve chest definition and upper body strength, and can enhance shoulder mobility when performed with proper form.
difficulty reason: It is classified as Intermediate because it requires dumbbells and involves a controlled, compound-like movement that improves strength and muscle tone.

name: hyperextension
instructions:
Adjust the hyperextension bench so that your upper thighs are resting on the pad and your feet are secured.
Cross your arms over your chest or place your hands behind your head.
Lower your upper body towards the ground while keeping your back straight.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then raise your upper body back up until it is in line with your legs.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The hyperextension targets the lower back, glutes, and hamstrings, strengthening the posterior chain and improving core stability. It helps correct posture and reduce lower‑back pain by increasing lumbar extension strength, making it useful for overall back health and athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It is a body‑weight exercise that requires minimal equipment and coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: hyperextension (on bench)
instructions:
Adjust the hyperextension bench so that your hips are resting comfortably on the pad and your feet are secured.
Cross your arms over your chest or place your hands behind your head.
Slowly lower your upper body towards the ground while keeping your back straight.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then raise your upper body back up until it is in line with your legs.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The hyperextension on a bench works the posterior chain, strengthening the spine, glutes, and hamstrings while improving core stability and posture. It also helps increase flexibility in the lower back and promotes balanced muscle development.
difficulty reason: It requires a bench and controlled movement, making it a compound body‑weight exercise that challenges coordination and core strength.

name: impossible dips
instructions:
Position yourself between two parallel bars with your arms fully extended and your body suspended in the air.
Bend your knees and cross your ankles.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are parallel to the ground.
Pause for a moment, then push yourself back up to the starting position by straightening your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the triceps, chest, and shoulders, engaging the entire upper body for strength and power. It improves muscular endurance, stabilizes the shoulder girdle, and enhances overall upper body strength.
difficulty reason: It demands significant upper-body strength, coordination, and core stability beyond standard bodyweight dips.

name: inchworm
instructions:
Start in a standing position with your feet hip-width apart.
Bend forward at the waist and place your hands on the ground in front of you.
Walk your hands forward until you are in a high plank position, with your body in a straight line from head to toe.
Pause for a moment, then walk your hands back towards your feet, keeping your legs as straight as possible.
Once your hands reach your feet, stand back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The inchworm targets the core, shoulders, and hamstrings, engaging the abs through a dynamic stretch and plank hold. It improves flexibility in the hamstrings and spine, strengthens the shoulders, and enhances core stability.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight exercise that requires minimal coordination and equipment, making it suitable for beginners.

name: inchworm v. 2
instructions:
Start in a standing position with your feet hip-width apart.
Bend forward at the waist and place your hands on the ground in front of you.
Walk your hands forward until you are in a high plank position, with your body in a straight line from head to toe.
Keeping your legs straight, walk your feet towards your hands, bringing your hips up towards the ceiling.
Continue walking your hands forward, repeating the movement for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The Inchworm v.2 engages the core, shoulders, and hamstrings through a dynamic stretch and plank transition. It improves abdominal stability, upper body flexibility, and hip mobility.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight and requires minimal coordination, making it accessible to beginners.

name: incline close-grip push-up
instructions:
Place your hands on an elevated surface, such as a bench or step, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Extend your legs behind you, resting on the balls of your feet, with your body forming a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest towards the elevated surface by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your sides.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push yourself back up to the starting position by straightening your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Incline close‑grip push‑ups target the triceps, chest, and shoulders while also engaging the core for stability. This variation reduces the load compared to a standard push‑up, making it easier to build upper‑body strength and improve muscular endurance. It’s an excellent starter move for developing triceps power and upper‑body conditioning.
difficulty reason: It’s a bodyweight exercise that requires minimal coordination and no equipment, fitting the beginner criteria.

name: incline leg hip raise (leg straight)
instructions:
Lie on an incline bench with your back flat against the bench and your legs extended straight out in front of you.
Place your hands on the sides of the bench for support.
Engaging your abs, lift your legs off the bench, raising them as high as you can while keeping them straight.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This incline leg hip raise works the hip flexors and rectus abdominis, strengthening core stability and improving hip flexion control. It also helps tone the lower abs and enhances posture by engaging the deep abdominal muscles. Consistent practice can improve overall core strength and support daily functional movements.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight exercise with a simple movement pattern that requires minimal coordination.

name: incline push up depth jump
instructions:
Find an elevated surface, such as a bench or step, and place your hands shoulder-width apart on the edge.
