description: The lever assisted chin‑up targets the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the biceps and forearms, helping build upper‑back strength and improve pulling mechanics. By using a lever for assistance, it allows beginners to develop proper form while still stimulating muscle growth. Over time, it enhances grip endurance and prepares the body for full chin‑ups.
difficulty reason: The movement is a bodyweight exercise with assistance, making it simple and low coordination, ideal for beginners.

name: lever back extension
instructions:
Adjust the machine to fit your body size and range of motion.
Sit on the machine with your back against the pad and your feet secured.
Place your hands on the handles or grip bars.
Engage your core and slowly lean forward, allowing your back to round slightly.
Pause for a moment at the bottom position, feeling a stretch in your lower back.
Using your back muscles, slowly raise your torso back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Lever back extensions target the spine, glutes, and hamstrings, helping to strengthen the posterior chain and improve lower back stability. The exercise also enhances core engagement and promotes better posture and spinal alignment.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine and involves a compound movement that requires coordination, but it is not as complex or high-resistance as advanced or expert-level exercises.

name: lever bent over row
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent.
Hold the barbell with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Bend forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and chest up.
Pull the barbell towards your lower chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, then slowly release and extend your arms back to the starting position.

description: The lever reverse T‑bar row targets the upper back, engaging the latissimus dorsi, rhomboids, and rear deltoids, while also working the biceps and forearms for a balanced pull. This exercise improves posture, strengthens the posterior chain, and enhances grip endurance.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine to perform a compound pull, requiring coordination and core stability but not advanced equipment or high resistance.

name: lever rotary calf
instructions:
Adjust the seat height so that your knees are slightly bent and your feet are flat on the footplate.
Place your toes on the footplate, with your heels hanging off the edge.
Grasp the handles or the sides of the machine for stability.
Push through the balls of your feet, raising your heels as high as possible.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever rotary calf exercise targets the calf muscles, focusing on the soleus and ankle stabilizers. It strengthens the lower leg, improves ankle stability, and enhances endurance for activities like running and jumping. The movement also promotes better calf muscle balance and reduces injury risk.
difficulty reason: This exercise uses a lever machine with minimal coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: lever seated calf press
instructions:
Adjust the seat of the machine so that your shoulders are aligned with the lever pad.
Place your toes on the lower portion of the platform and position your knees under the lever pad.
Grasp the handles on the sides of the seat for stability.
Press the lever pad down by extending your ankles, lifting your heels as high as possible.
Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the calves, focusing on the soleus and gastrocnemius, strengthening ankle extensors and improving lower‑leg stability. It also engages the hamstrings for control and helps develop explosive power for running and jumping.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine and requires coordination but not advanced free‑weight skills.

name: lever seated calf raise
instructions:
Adjust the seat height so that your knees are slightly bent and your feet are flat on the footplate.
Place your toes on the footplate with your heels hanging off the edge.
Grasp the handles or the sides of the seat for stability.
Push through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated calf raise isolates the calf muscles, especially the soleus and ankle stabilizers, allowing for a focused contraction and improved calf strength and endurance. It helps develop a fuller, more defined calf and improves ankle stability for better performance in sports and daily activities.
difficulty reason: This exercise uses a lever machine which provides a controlled resistance for calf development, making it suitable for intermediate lifters.

name: lever seated crunch
instructions:
Sit on the leverage machine with your back against the pad and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your hands on the side pads for support.
Engage your abs and slowly lean back, allowing the pad to move with you.
Once your upper body is at a 45-degree angle, contract your abs and crunch forward, bringing your chest towards your knees.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly release and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated crunch targets the abdominal muscles, especially the obliques, helping to strengthen core stability and improve trunk flexion. It also engages the lower abs and hip flexors, promoting better posture and core endurance.
difficulty reason: The movement is a simple, seated core exercise using a leverage machine, requiring minimal coordination and equipment.

name: lever seated crunch (chest pad)
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and chest pad position according to your comfort.
Sit on the machine with your back against the chest pad and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or side bars for stability.
Engage your abs and slowly lean back, allowing the chest pad to move with you.
Pause for a moment at the maximum contraction, feeling the tension in your abs.
Slowly return to the starting position by contracting your abs and pulling yourself back up.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated crunch targets the rectus abdominis and obliques, helping to strengthen the core and improve posture. It also engages the lower back and hip flexors for a balanced abdominal workout. Consistent practice can increase core stability and support daily movements.
difficulty reason: It is a straightforward machine-assisted crunch that requires minimal coordination and uses bodyweight with simple lever control.

name: lever seated crunch v. 2
instructions:
Sit on the leverage machine with your back against the pad and your feet secured under the footpads.
Place your hands on the handles or the sides of the seat for support.
Engage your abs and slowly crunch forward, bringing your chest towards your knees.
Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, squeezing your abs.
Slowly return to the starting position, allowing your back to round slightly.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated crunch v.2 targets the core, specifically the obliques and hip flexors, while engaging the entire abdominal wall. By using controlled, progressive resistance, it improves core stability, strengthens the waist, and helps protect the lower back during daily activities.
difficulty reason: It employs a leverage machine for a controlled ab exercise, requiring basic equipment and moderate coordination, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lever seated dip
instructions:
Adjust the seat height so that your feet are flat on the ground and your knees are at a 90-degree angle.
Grasp the handles of the leverage machine with your palms facing down and your arms fully extended.
Slowly lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are parallel to the ground.
Pause for a moment, then push yourself back up to the starting position by straightening your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Lever seated dips target the triceps, while also engaging the chest and shoulders for stability and upper‑body strength. This exercise improves arm power, enhances shoulder stability, and helps develop a stronger, more defined upper body.
difficulty reason: Because it requires a lever machine and coordination to perform a compound movement that works multiple upper‑body muscles.

name: lever seated fly
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your back against the pad.
Grasp the handles with a pronated grip and keep your elbows slightly bent.
Exhale and push the handles forward, bringing them together in front of your chest.
Pause for a moment, squeezing your chest muscles.
Inhale and slowly return to the starting position, allowing your chest muscles to stretch.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated fly targets the pectoral muscles while also engaging the anterior deltoids and upper trapezius for stability. It helps build chest strength and definition, improve posture, and enhance shoulder joint mobility by performing a controlled adduction movement.
difficulty reason: The exercise uses a basic lever machine and requires coordination of the shoulder and chest muscles, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lever seated good morning
instructions:
Adjust the seat height so that your hips are slightly higher than your knees.
Sit on the machine with your back against the pad and your feet flat on the footrests.
Grasp the handles or the sides of the seat for stability.
Keeping your back straight, slowly lean forward from your hips until your upper body is parallel to the ground.
Pause for a moment, then slowly return to the starting position by pushing through your glutes and hamstrings.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated good morning targets the glutes, hamstrings, and lower back, engaging the posterior chain. It improves hip extension strength, enhances core stability, and can help prevent lower back pain when performed correctly.
difficulty reason: Uses a lever machine to perform a compound hip hinge, requiring moderate coordination and equipment.

name: lever seated hip abduction
instructions:
Adjust the seat height so that your knees are at a 90-degree angle.
Sit on the machine with your back against the backrest and your feet on the footrests.
Place your hands on the side handles for stability.
Engage your abductors and slowly push your legs apart, away from the midline of your body.
Pause for a moment at the end of the movement, then slowly bring your legs back together to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Target the abductors, glutes, and hamstrings by lifting your legs away from the midline while seated. This exercise strengthens hip stability, improves gait mechanics, and helps prevent injury. It also enhances core balance through controlled movement.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine and requires minimal coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: lever seated hip adduction
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your back against the backrest.
Place your feet on the footrests and grasp the handles for stability.
Engage your adductor muscles and slowly bring your legs together, squeezing your inner thighs.
Pause for a moment at the peak contraction, then slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated hip adduction targets the adductor muscles of the upper legs, strengthening the inner thigh and improving hip stability. By engaging the adductors through controlled movement, it enhances muscular endurance and supports proper gait mechanics.
difficulty reason: It requires a lever machine and coordination to maintain form, making it more suitable for intermediate users.

name: lever seated leg curl
instructions:
Adjust the machine to fit your body and sit on it with your back against the backrest.
Place your lower legs under the padded lever, just above your ankles.
Grasp the handles on the sides of the machine for support.
Keeping your upper legs stationary, exhale and curl your legs up as far as possible.
Hold the contracted position for a brief pause as you squeeze your hamstrings.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated leg curl targets the hamstrings, helping to strengthen the knee flexors and improve posterior chain stability. It also engages the calves as secondary movers, aiding in ankle flexion and overall lower‑leg endurance. Consistent use can enhance athletic performance and reduce injury risk.
difficulty reason: It uses a simple lever machine with bodyweight, requiring minimal coordination and equipment.

name: lever seated leg raise crunch
instructions:
Sit on the leverage machine with your back against the backrest and your feet on the footrests.
Grasp the handles or sidebars for stability.
Engage your abs and slowly raise your legs up towards your chest, curling your torso forward at the same time.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs and torso back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated leg raise crunch targets the abdominal muscles and hip flexors, helping to strengthen the core and improve trunk stability. It also enhances lumbar flexibility and promotes better posture.
difficulty reason: This exercise uses a leverage machine and involves coordinated torso and leg movement, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lever seated reverse fly
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your chest against the pad and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and keep your arms slightly bent.
Exhale and squeeze your shoulder blades together as you pull the handles back and outward, away from your body.
Pause for a moment at the peak contraction, then inhale and slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated reverse fly targets the rear deltoids while engaging the trapezius and rhomboids for scapular retraction. This movement improves shoulder stability, posture, and upper back strength by promoting balanced development of the posterior shoulder girdle.
difficulty reason: It utilizes a lever machine and requires coordination of shoulder blade retraction, making it suitable for those with basic equipment experience.

name: lever seated reverse fly (parallel grip)
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your chest against the pad and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles with a parallel grip, palms facing each other, and keep your arms slightly bent.
Exhale and squeeze your shoulder blades together as you pull the handles back and outward, away from your body.
Pause for a moment at the peak contraction, then inhale and slowly return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated reverse fly targets the rear deltoids, trapezius, and rhomboids, helping to strengthen the upper back and improve posture. By pulling the handles back and outward with a slight bend, it isolates the posterior shoulder complex while also engaging stabilizers for balanced shoulder development.
difficulty reason: This exercise uses a lever machine for controlled movement, fitting the intermediate category of compound machine work.

name: lever seated row
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and footrests to a comfortable position.
Sit on the machine with your chest against the pad and your feet on the footrests.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip, shoulder-width apart.
Keep your back straight and your core engaged.
Pull the handles towards your body, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the peak of the movement.
Slowly release the handles and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever seated row targets the upper back, engaging the rhomboids, middle trapezius, and latissimus dorsi while also working the biceps and forearms. It improves posture, strengthens the posterior chain, and enhances pulling power for daily activities and sports.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic lever machine for a compound pulling movement that requires moderate coordination and strength.

name: lever seated squat calf raise on leg press machine
instructions:
Adjust the seat of the leg press machine so that your knees are slightly bent when your feet are on the footplate.
Sit on the machine with your back against the backrest and your feet flat on the footplate, shoulder-width apart.
Place your toes and the balls of your feet on the footplate, keeping your heels off the edge.
Release the safety handles and push the footplate away from you by extending your knees.
Once your knees are fully extended, slowly lower your heels by flexing your calves.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push the footplate back up by extending your calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This lever seated squat calf raise targets the calves, while also engaging the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes for a balanced lower‑body workout. It improves calf strength, ankle stability, and overall leg power.
difficulty reason: It utilizes a basic leg press machine and combines knee extension with calf flexion, requiring moderate coordination and equipment use.

name: lever shoulder press
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your back against the backrest.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and position your hands at shoulder level.
Push the handles upward until your arms are fully extended, but do not lock your elbows.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the handles back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever shoulder press targets the deltoids while also engaging the triceps and chest, helping to build upper‑body strength and improve shoulder stability.
difficulty reason: It’s a machine‑based compound movement that requires moderate coordination and uses a lever system, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lever shoulder press v. 2
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and backrest of the leverage machine to a comfortable position.
Sit on the machine with your back against the backrest and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles of the machine with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Push the handles upward and forward until your arms are fully extended, but not locked.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the handles back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever shoulder press targets the deltoids while also engaging the triceps and chest, helping to build overall shoulder strength, improve upper‑body stability, and enhance pushing power. This machine‑based exercise allows controlled movement, reducing injury risk and enabling progressive overload for consistent muscle growth.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine for a compound shoulder movement that requires moderate coordination and resistance, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lever shoulder press v. 3
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and backrest of the leverage machine to a comfortable position.
Sit on the machine with your back against the backrest and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles of the machine with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Push the handles upward and forward until your arms are fully extended, but not locked.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the handles back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever shoulder press targets the deltoids while also engaging the triceps and upper chest. It builds shoulder strength and stability, improving overhead pressing performance and posture.
difficulty reason: It requires a lever machine and basic coordination, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lever shrug
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the leverage machine with your back against the pad.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and keep your arms straight.
Keeping your back straight, lift your shoulders up towards your ears as high as possible.
Hold the contraction for a moment, then slowly lower your shoulders back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Lever shrugs target the trapezius and upper shoulder muscles, helping to strengthen the upper back and improve posture while also enhancing shoulder stability.
difficulty reason: The lever shrug uses a specialized lever machine for a compound movement that is straightforward yet effective for intermediate lifters.

name: lever standing calf raise
instructions:
Adjust the machine to your height and stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Place your shoulders under the pads and hold onto the handles for stability.
Raise your heels as high as possible by extending your ankles.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heels back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever standing calf raise targets the calves, especially the soleus and ankle stabilizers, helping to build lower-leg strength, improve ankle stability, and enhance power for running and jumping.
difficulty reason: Using a lever machine provides controlled, progressive overload while the movement remains straightforward, making it suitable for intermediate lifters.

name: lever standing chest press
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your feet flat on the ground.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and position your hands at chest level.
Push the handles forward until your arms are fully extended, keeping your elbows slightly bent.
Pause for a moment, then slowly bring the handles back towards your chest, maintaining control throughout the movement.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This lever standing chest press targets the pectoral muscles, engaging the anterior deltoids and triceps for a full upper‑body push. It improves chest strength, shoulder stability, and functional pushing power while allowing controlled resistance through the lever system.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine and involves a compound movement, suitable for those with some basic equipment experience.

name: lever t bar row
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and footplate position to ensure proper alignment.
Sit on the machine with your chest against the pad and your feet flat on the footplate.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your back straight and engage your core.
Pull the handles towards your torso, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the peak contraction, then slowly release the handles back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever T‑bar row targets the upper back, engaging the latissimus dorsi, rhomboids, and trapezius while also working the biceps and forearms. It improves posture, strengthens the posterior chain, and enhances pulling strength for functional activities.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine for a compound pulling movement, fitting the intermediate difficulty level.

name: lever t-bar reverse grip row
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your chest against the pad and your feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your back straight and engage your core.
Pull the handles towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, then slowly release and extend your arms back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever T‑bar reverse grip row targets the upper back, engaging the rhomboids, middle trapezius, and lats, while the reverse grip also works the biceps and forearms. This compound movement improves posture, strengthens the pulling muscles, and enhances overall back thickness and stability.
difficulty reason: It uses a lever machine and requires coordination to maintain a straight back, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lever triceps extension
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your back against the pad.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and fully extend your arms in front of you.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, slowly lower the handles towards your forehead by bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push the handles back up to the starting position by extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever triceps extension isolates the triceps brachii, helping to build arm strength, improve muscle definition, and support pushing motions in daily activities.
difficulty reason: The exercise uses a simple lever machine and requires minimal coordination, making it accessible for beginners.

name: lever unilateral row
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself facing the machine.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and keep your back straight.
Pull the handles towards your body, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the peak of the movement, then slowly release and extend your arms back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lever unilateral row targets the upper back, engaging the rhomboids, trapezius, and latissimus dorsi, while also working the biceps and forearms for a balanced pulling motion. This exercise improves posture, strengthens the posterior chain, and enhances grip endurance. By isolating each side, it also corrects muscular imbalances and promotes symmetrical development.
difficulty reason: It requires a basic lever machine and coordination to perform a compound row with a controlled single‑leg stance.

name: london bridge
instructions:
Attach the rope to a high anchor point.
Stand facing away from the anchor point with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the rope with an overhand grip, palms facing down.
Lean forward slightly, keeping your back straight and core engaged.
Pull the rope towards your body, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the peak of the movement, then slowly release the tension and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This rope pull exercise targets the upper back, engaging the latissimus dorsi, rhomboids, and rear deltoids while also working the biceps and forearms. It strengthens the pulling muscles and improves posture and shoulder stability.
difficulty reason: The movement uses a rope and a high anchor, requiring moderate coordination and equipment, fitting the intermediate level.

name: low glute bridge on floor
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your arms by your sides, palms facing down.
Engage your glutes and core, then lift your hips off the ground until your body forms a straight line from your knees to your shoulders.
Pause for a moment at the top, squeezing your glutes.
Slowly lower your hips back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the glutes, hamstrings, and core, helping to strengthen hip extension and improve lower back stability. By engaging these muscles, it can enhance posture and support athletic performance. The movement also aids in preventing common lower back pain.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight with a simple movement that requires minimal coordination.

name: lower back curl
instructions:
Lie flat on your stomach with your legs extended and your arms by your sides.
Engage your glutes and hamstrings, and slowly lift your upper body off the ground, curling your back upwards.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lower back curl targets the spine while engaging the glutes and hamstrings, strengthening the posterior chain and improving core stability. It helps alleviate lower back tension, enhance posture, and support spinal health. This bodyweight exercise is a simple way to build endurance in the lower back and glutes.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight exercise with a simple movement pattern and minimal coordination required.

name: lunge with jump
instructions:
Start by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Take a step forward with your right foot, lowering your body into a lunge position.
Push off with your right foot and jump into the air, switching the position of your feet mid-air.
Land softly with your left foot forward and immediately lower your body into a lunge position.
Continue alternating between lunges and jumps for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Lunge with jump is a dynamic plyometric move that targets the glutes, quadriceps, hamstrings, and calves, improving lower‑body power, balance, and cardiovascular fitness. By alternating lunges and explosive jumps, you develop coordination and muscular endurance while also boosting your heart rate.
difficulty reason: It is a body‑weight compound exercise that requires coordination and power, fitting the intermediate level.

name: lunge with twist
instructions:
Start by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Take a step forward with your right foot, lowering your body into a lunge position.
As you lunge, twist your torso to the right, bringing your left elbow towards your right knee.
Pause for a moment, then return to the starting position.
Repeat on the other side, stepping forward with your left foot and twisting your torso to the left.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lunge with twist works the quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, and engages the abdominal muscles for core stability. It improves lower‑body strength, balance, and rotational mobility while also boosting cardiovascular endurance when performed at a brisk pace.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight and involves straightforward movements with minimal coordination demands.

name: lying (side) quads stretch
instructions:
Lie on your side with your legs straight.
Bend your top leg and grab your ankle or foot with your hand.
Gently pull your ankle or foot towards your glutes until you feel a stretch in your quads.
Hold the stretch for 20-30 seconds.
Release the stretch and repeat on the other side.
description: This lying side quad stretch targets the quadriceps on the front of the thighs, helping to improve flexibility and reduce tightness. It also gently engages the glutes and lower back for a balanced stretch, promoting better hip mobility and overall lower‑body comfort.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight and simple movements, requiring minimal coordination.

name: lying elbow to knee
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engage your abs and lift your upper body off the ground, bringing your right elbow towards your left knee.
At the same time, extend your right leg straight out and lift it off the ground.
Pause for a moment, then return to the starting position.
Repeat the movement, this time bringing your left elbow towards your right knee and extending your left leg.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This bodyweight core exercise targets the abs, hip flexors, and lower back, strengthening the core and improving stability. It also enhances rotational control and engages the obliques for balanced development.
difficulty reason: It requires bodyweight coordination and core strength, making it suitable for intermediate-level trainees.

name: lying leg raise flat bench
instructions:
Lie flat on a flat bench with your back pressed against it.
Place your hands under your glutes for support.
Keep your legs straight and together, and lift them up towards the ceiling.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lying leg raise on a flat bench targets the abdominal core and hip flexors, strengthening the lower abs and improving core stability. It also helps develop control and endurance in the hip flexors, supporting better posture and athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It requires no equipment and minimal coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: lying leg-hip raise
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your legs extended and your arms by your sides.
Place your hands under your glutes for support.
Engage your core and lift your legs off the ground, raising them towards the ceiling.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your legs back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The lying leg-hip raise targets the lower abs and hip flexors, strengthening core stability and enhancing hip flexion strength for better posture and athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It is a simple bodyweight exercise requiring minimal coordination, making it ideal for beginners.

name: machine inner chest press
instructions:
Adjust the seat height and position yourself on the machine with your back flat against the pad.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and position your elbows at a 90-degree angle.
Push the handles forward until your arms are fully extended, exhaling during the movement.
Pause for a moment at the end of the movement, then slowly return to the starting position, inhaling as you do so.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The machine inner chest press targets the inner pectoral fibers, while also engaging the triceps and anterior deltoids. It helps build chest strength and shape, improving upper body power and stability.
difficulty reason: Because it uses a lever machine for controlled resistance and requires coordination of chest, triceps, and shoulders.

name: march sit (wall)
instructions:
Stand with your back against a wall and your feet hip-width apart.
Slowly slide your back down the wall until your knees are bent at a 90-degree angle.
Lift your right foot off the ground and bring your knee towards your chest.
Lower your right foot back down and lift your left foot off the ground, bringing your knee towards your chest.
Continue alternating between lifting your right and left foot for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The wall‑march sit targets the glutes, quadriceps, hamstrings, and calves, helping to strengthen the entire lower body while engaging the core for stability. By alternating knee lifts against a wall, it improves hip mobility and endurance, making it ideal for beginners looking to build foundational strength.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight and a wall, requiring minimal coordination and making it accessible to those new to resistance training.

name: medicine ball catch and overhead throw
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball in both hands at chest level.
Bend your knees slightly and engage your core.
Lower your body into a squat position, keeping your back straight and chest up.
Explosively extend your hips and legs, while simultaneously throwing the medicine ball overhead.
Release the ball at the top of the movement and catch it on the way down.
Lower your body back into the squat position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This medicine ball catch and overhead throw targets the lats, shoulders, and core, helping to build upper body power and stability while improving coordination and explosive strength. It also engages the lower body through the squat component, promoting functional movement and overall athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It combines basic equipment with a compound movement that requires coordination and moderate intensity.

name: medicine ball chest pass
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding the medicine ball at chest level.
Extend your arms forward, pushing the medicine ball away from your chest with force.
As you release the ball, follow through with your arms and torso, transferring your weight from your back foot to your front foot.
Catch the ball as it rebounds off the wall or partner, and immediately repeat the movement.
Continue for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The medicine ball chest pass targets the pectoral muscles while also engaging the shoulders and triceps for a powerful pushing motion. It enhances upper‑body strength, improves explosive power, and boosts coordination and core stability as you transfer weight through the movement.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment (medicine ball) and a compound push movement that requires moderate coordination and strength.

name: medicine ball chest push from 3 point stance
instructions:
Start in a 3 point stance with one hand on the medicine ball and the other hand on the ground.
Extend your legs and position your body in a straight line.
Lower your chest towards the ground while keeping your back straight.
Push the medicine ball away from your body, extending your arm fully.
Return to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Targets the pectorals while engaging the shoulders and triceps for a full chest push, improving upper body strength and stability. The movement also enhances core control and balance from a 3‑point stance.
difficulty reason: Requires a medicine ball and coordination for the 3‑point push, fitting the intermediate category.

name: medicine ball chest push multiple response
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest level.
Extend your arms forward, pushing the medicine ball away from your chest.
Pause for a moment, then slowly bring the medicine ball back to your chest.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The medicine ball chest push works the pectoral muscles while also engaging the triceps and shoulders, improving upper‑body pushing strength and stability.
difficulty reason: It’s a straightforward body‑weight movement with a light medicine ball, requiring minimal coordination and equipment.

name: medicine ball chest push single response
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding the medicine ball at chest level.
Extend your arms forward, pushing the medicine ball away from your chest.
Pause for a moment, then slowly bring the medicine ball back to your chest.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The medicine ball chest push targets the pectoralis major while also engaging triceps and anterior deltoids for upper body strength and stability. It improves push strength, core stability, and helps develop explosive power in the chest and shoulders.
difficulty reason: It uses only a medicine ball and a simple push motion, making it accessible for beginners.

name: medicine ball chest push with run release
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest level.
Step forward with your right foot and simultaneously push the medicine ball forward, extending your arms fully.
As you push the ball forward, release it and let it roll forward.
Quickly run forward and catch the ball before it hits the ground.
Once you catch the ball, bring it back to your chest and repeat the movement with your left foot forward.
Continue alternating legs and repeating the exercise for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This dynamic movement targets the pectoral muscles while engaging triceps, shoulders, and core for stability and power. It enhances upper body pushing strength, improves core coordination, and boosts cardiovascular conditioning due to the running component.
difficulty reason: It requires a medicine ball and coordination between pushing and running, fitting the intermediate category.

name: medicine ball close grip push up
instructions:
Start in a high plank position with your hands on the medicine ball, shoulder-width apart.
Lower your body towards the ground by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your sides.
Push back up to the starting position, fully extending your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This medicine ball close grip push up targets the triceps, chest, and shoulders while also engaging core stability. It improves upper body strength, muscular endurance, and enhances functional pushing power. The added instability of the medicine ball challenges balance and recruits stabilizer muscles, aiding overall functional performance.
difficulty reason: It requires coordination and core stability beyond a standard push up, making it suitable for intermediate training.

name: medicine ball overhead slam
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball with both hands above your head.
Engage your core and keep your back straight.
Bend your knees slightly and forcefully slam the medicine ball down to the ground in front of you.
As you slam the ball down, use your entire body to generate power, including your shoulders and core.
Catch the ball on the bounce and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The medicine ball overhead slam activates the upper back, shoulders, and core, improving explosive power and stability. It also enhances coordination and cardiovascular endurance while engaging multiple muscle groups simultaneously.
difficulty reason: It requires a medicine ball and dynamic movement that demands core engagement and coordination beyond basic bodyweight exercises.

name: medicine ball supine chest throw
instructions:
Lie flat on your back on a bench with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Hold the medicine ball with both hands, extending your arms straight up above your chest.
Lower the medicine ball towards your chest, keeping your elbows close to your body.
Explosively push the medicine ball upwards, extending your arms fully and throwing the ball as high as possible.
Catch the medicine ball and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the triceps, chest, and shoulders, helping to build upper‑body power and stability. By explosively extending the arms, it also improves coordination and core engagement.
difficulty reason: It involves a medicine ball and requires controlled explosive movement, making it more advanced than basic bodyweight drills but not as complex as specialized equipment exercises.

name: mixed grip chin-up
instructions:
Grab the pull-up bar with an underhand grip (palms facing towards you) and your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hang from the bar with your arms fully extended and your feet off the ground.
Engage your back muscles and pull your body up towards the bar, leading with your chest.
Continue pulling until your chin is above the bar.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your body back down to the starting position with control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the lats, biceps, and forearms, strengthening the upper back and pulling muscles. It improves grip endurance and overall upper body pulling strength, aiding in better performance for other pulling movements.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight compound movement that requires significant upper body strength and grip coordination.

name: modified hindu push-up (male)
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and your feet hip-width apart.
Lower your body towards the ground, bending your elbows and keeping your core engaged.
As you lower your body, shift your weight back and lift your hips up towards the ceiling, creating an inverted V shape with your body.
Continue to lower your body until your chest is just above the ground, then reverse the movement, pushing your hips back down and extending your arms to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The modified Hindu push-up targets the pectorals while also engaging the triceps, shoulders, and core for a comprehensive upper‑body and core workout. It improves muscular endurance, enhances shoulder stability, and promotes a stronger core and better posture.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight exercise that adds a hip‑lift movement requiring core stability and coordination beyond a basic push‑up.

name: modified push up to lower arms
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands directly under your shoulders and your body in a straight line.
Lower your body down towards the ground by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your sides.
Once your elbows are at a 90-degree angle, lower your forearms to the ground, keeping your elbows directly under your shoulders.
Pause for a moment, then push through your palms to lift your forearms back up to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This modified push-up targets the forearms, triceps, chest, and shoulders, helping to build upper-body strength and improve wrist stability. It also enhances core stability as you maintain a straight body line, and can be performed anywhere without equipment.
difficulty reason: It relies solely on body weight and simple elbow movements, requiring minimal coordination and making it suitable for beginners.

name: monster walk
instructions:
Place a resistance band around your ankles.
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and slightly bend your knees.
Take a step to the side with your right foot, maintaining tension on the resistance band.
Follow with your left foot, stepping to the side to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides.
description: Monster walks target the glutes, hamstrings, and quadriceps, strengthening the hip abductors and improving lower‑body stability. They also help build muscular endurance and enhance functional movement patterns.
difficulty reason: It is a simple bodyweight exercise that can be performed with a resistance band and requires minimal coordination.

name: mountain climber
instructions:
Start in a high plank position with your hands directly under your shoulders and your body in a straight line.
Engage your core and bring your right knee towards your chest, then quickly switch and bring your left knee towards your chest.
Continue alternating legs in a running motion, keeping your hips low and your core engaged.
Maintain a steady pace and breathe evenly throughout the exercise.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Mountain climbers target the core, shoulders, and triceps while simultaneously elevating heart rate. This bodyweight exercise improves cardiovascular endurance, core stability, and upper-body strength. It also enhances coordination and agility.
difficulty reason: It requires only body weight and simple movements, making it accessible for beginners.

name: muscle up
instructions:
Start by hanging from a pull-up bar with your palms facing away from you and your arms fully extended.
Engage your core and pull your body up towards the bar, leading with your chest.
As you reach the top of the movement, transition your grip so that your palms are facing towards you.
Continue pulling yourself up until your chest is above the bar and your arms are fully flexed.
Reverse the movement by slowly lowering yourself back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The muscle‑up targets the lats, biceps, triceps, shoulders, and chest, engaging the entire upper body for a powerful pulling and pushing motion. It builds explosive strength, improves grip endurance, and enhances overall upper‑body stability and coordination.
difficulty reason: It demands high upper‑body strength, explosive power, and complex coordination, making it suitable only for advanced trainees.

name: muscle-up (on vertical bar)
instructions:
Start by hanging from a vertical bar with your palms facing away from you and your arms fully extended.
Engage your core and pull your body up towards the bar, leading with your chest.
As you pull yourself up, lean back slightly and bring your elbows towards your sides.
Continue pulling until your chest reaches the bar and your elbows are fully bent.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower yourself back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: A muscle‑up on a vertical bar works the lats, chest, back, biceps, triceps and forearms, building upper‑body pulling power and explosive strength while improving coordination and core stability.
difficulty reason: It combines a powerful pull with a transition to a dip, demanding both strength and refined technique.

name: narrow push-up on exercise ball
instructions:
Place the stability ball on the ground and position yourself in a push-up position with your hands on the ball, slightly narrower than shoulder-width apart.
Engage your core and keep your body in a straight line from head to toe.
Lower your chest towards the ball by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your body.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push yourself back up to the starting position by straightening your arms.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the triceps, chest, and shoulders while engaging the core for stability. It improves upper body strength, shoulder stability, and core control.
difficulty reason: It requires balance on a stability ball and coordination for a narrow push‑up, making it more challenging than a standard body‑weight push‑up but not involving heavy or advanced equipment.

name: neck side stretch
instructions:
Stand or sit up straight with your shoulders relaxed.
Tilt your head to one side, bringing your ear towards your shoulder.
Hold the stretch for 15-30 seconds.
Repeat on the other side.
Perform 2-4 sets on each side.
description: The neck side stretch targets the levator scapulae, trapezius, and sternocleidomastoid, helping to improve neck flexibility and reduce tension while promoting better posture.
difficulty reason: It requires only bodyweight and simple head tilting, making it easy to perform with low coordination.

name: negative crunch
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engaging your abs, slowly lift your upper body off the ground, curling forward until your torso is at a 45-degree angle.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Negative crunches target the abdominal muscles, especially the rectus abdominis and hip flexors, helping to strengthen core stability and improve posture. They also enhance the mind–body connection by emphasizing controlled eccentric movement, which can increase muscle endurance.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight and involves a basic ab movement that requires minimal equipment and coordination.

name: oblique crunch v. 2
instructions:
Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head or cross them over your chest.
Engage your abs and lift your shoulder blades off the ground, rotating your torso to one side.
Pause for a moment, then lower your shoulder blades back down to the starting position.
Repeat on the other side.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the obliques, helping to strengthen the core and improve rotational stability. It also engages the rectus abdominis and hip flexors for overall abdominal conditioning.
difficulty reason: Bodyweight, simple, low coordination.

name: oblique crunches floor
instructions:
Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Place your hands behind your head or cross them over your chest.
Engage your abs and lift your shoulder blades off the floor, rotating your torso to one side.
Pause for a moment, then lower your shoulder blades back down to the floor.
Repeat on the other side, alternating sides with each repetition.
description: Oblique crunches target the oblique muscles on the sides of the abdomen, helping to strengthen the core and improve rotational stability. This exercise also promotes better posture and can aid in reducing lower back strain.
difficulty reason: It requires no equipment and involves simple torso rotation, making it suitable for beginners.

name: olympic barbell hammer curl
instructions:
Stand up straight with your feet shoulder-width apart and hold an Olympic barbell with an overhand grip.
Let the barbell hang at arm's length in front of your thighs, with your palms facing your body.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, exhale and curl the weights while contracting your biceps.
Continue to raise the barbell until your biceps are fully contracted and the barbell is at shoulder level.
Hold the contracted position for a brief pause as you squeeze your biceps.
Inhale and slowly begin to lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The Olympic barbell hammer curl targets the biceps brachii and brachialis while also engaging the forearm flexors. This exercise builds arm strength, improves muscle endurance, and enhances grip stability for functional movements. It’s a versatile movement that can be incorporated into both strength and hypertrophy training programs.
difficulty reason: It uses a basic piece of equipment (Olympic barbell) and involves a compound lifting motion suitable for intermediate lifters.

name: olympic barbell triceps extension
instructions:
Start by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart and holding the barbell with an overhand grip.
Raise the barbell above your head, fully extending your arms.
Keeping your upper arms close to your head, slowly lower the barbell behind your head by bending your elbows.
Pause for a moment, then extend your arms back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The Olympic barbell triceps extension targets the triceps brachii, helping to build arm strength and definition. It also engages the shoulder stabilizers and core for balance during the overhead movement. Consistent practice improves upper‑arm power and supports daily pushing motions.
difficulty reason: It uses a barbell and requires coordination for an overhead movement, fitting the intermediate category.

name: one arm against wall
instructions:
Stand facing a wall with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Extend one arm straight out in front of you and place your palm against the wall.
Engage your core and lean your body forward, keeping your arm straight and your back flat.
Slowly push against the wall with your palm, activating your lat muscles.
Hold the position for a few seconds, then release and repeat with the other arm.
description: This bodyweight exercise targets the latissimus dorsi by engaging the back muscles while maintaining a stable core, helping improve posture and upper body strength.
difficulty reason: It uses only bodyweight and requires minimal coordination.

name: one arm chin-up
instructions:
Stand facing a pull-up bar with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Reach up and grab the bar with an underhand grip, with one hand gripping the bar and the other hand holding your wrist for support.
Hang from the bar with your arm fully extended, keeping your body straight and your core engaged.
Pull yourself up towards the bar by bending your elbow and squeezing your back muscles.
Continue pulling until your chin is above the bar, then slowly lower yourself back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms and repeat.
description: The one‑arm chin‑up targets the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the biceps and forearm flexors for a powerful pulling motion. It builds unilateral back strength, improves grip endurance, and enhances core stability and shoulder stability.
difficulty reason: Requires significant unilateral upper‑body strength, core stability, and coordination beyond basic bodyweight pulls.

name: one arm dip
instructions:
Stand facing away from a bench or chair, with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Place one hand on the bench or chair behind you, fingers pointing towards your body.
Extend your legs out in front of you, keeping your heels on the ground.
Bend your elbows and lower your body towards the ground, keeping your back close to the bench or chair.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push through your palms to straighten your arms and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides and repeat with the other arm.
description: This unilateral bodyweight dip targets the triceps while engaging the chest and shoulders for stabilization, improving upper‑arm strength and balance. It also enhances core stability due to the single‑leg stance.
difficulty reason: It demands balance and unilateral strength, making it more complex than typical body‑weight exercises.

name: one arm slam (with medicine ball)
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding the medicine ball with one hand in front of your waist.
Bend your knees slightly and engage your core.
Raise the medicine ball above your head, fully extending your arm.
Forcefully slam the medicine ball down to the ground, using your core and shoulders to generate power.
Catch the medicine ball on the bounce and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The One‑Arm Medicine Ball Slam targets the core, especially the abs, while also engaging the shoulders and upper back for a powerful, explosive movement. It helps build rotational strength, improves core stability, and boosts overall athletic performance by training the kinetic chain from the waist to the arms.
difficulty reason: It requires a medicine ball and coordination to perform the slam, fitting the intermediate category.

name: one arm towel row
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, and hold a towel with one hand.
Bend forward at the waist, keeping your back straight and your core engaged.
Extend your arm fully, allowing the towel to hang in front of you.
Pull the towel towards your chest, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower the towel back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch arms.
description: The one‑arm towel row targets the upper back, engaging the rhomboids, middle trapezius, and lats while also working the biceps and forearms. It improves pulling strength, posture, and unilateral balance, helping to correct muscle imbalances and enhance functional strength.
difficulty reason: It uses bodyweight and a single‑arm movement that demands moderate coordination and core stability.

name: one leg donkey calf raise
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing forward.
Place your hands on a stable surface for support, such as a wall or a bar.
Lift one leg off the ground, keeping your knee slightly bent.
Raise your heel as high as possible, using your calf muscles.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heel back down.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch legs.
description: The one‑leg donkey calf raise targets the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles of the calves, improving ankle stability and ankle dorsiflexion. It also engages the hamstrings and glutes for balance and core stability, helping to enhance lower‑leg strength and overall functional movement.
difficulty reason: It uses only body weight and requires minimal coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: one leg floor calf raise
instructions:
Stand with your feet hip-width apart and place your hands on a wall or sturdy object for balance.
Lift one foot off the ground and balance on the other foot.
Slowly raise your heel off the ground, lifting your body up onto the ball of your foot.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your heel back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch legs and repeat.
description: The one‑leg floor calf raise targets the calf muscles—gastrocnemius and soleus—while also engaging the ankle stabilizers in the foot. It builds ankle strength, improves balance and calf endurance, and can help prevent common lower‑leg injuries. The exercise is simple to perform with no equipment, making it ideal for beginners.
difficulty reason: It requires only body weight and a basic balance stance, making it low‑coordination and easy for beginners.

name: one leg squat
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Extend one leg forward, keeping it off the ground.
Bend your standing leg and lower your body down as if sitting back into a chair.
Keep your chest up and your back straight.
Push through your heel to return to the starting position.
Repeat with the other leg.
description: The one‑leg squat targets the glutes, quadriceps, hamstrings and calves, improving single‑leg strength and balance. It also enhances core stability and hip mobility, making it great for athletic performance and injury prevention.
difficulty reason: It requires unilateral strength, balance, and coordination, placing it beyond basic bodyweight moves but not needing equipment.

name: otis up
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engaging your abs, slowly lift your upper body off the ground, curling forward until your torso is at a 45-degree angle.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This weighted crunch targets the rectus abdominis, hip flexors, and lower back, strengthening the core and improving spinal stability. By lifting the torso with added resistance, it enhances abdominal endurance and helps sculpt a defined waistline.
difficulty reason: The exercise uses a weighted bar for added resistance, making it a basic equipment compound movement suitable for intermediate trainees.

name: outside leg kick push-up
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and your feet together.
Lower your body towards the ground by bending your elbows, keeping your back straight and core engaged.
As you push back up, kick one leg out to the side, extending it fully and engaging your glutes.
Return your leg to the starting position and repeat the push-up, alternating legs with each repetition.
Continue alternating leg kicks and push-ups for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This movement combines a standard push‑up with a lateral leg kick, engaging the core, chest and arms while targeting the glutes, quadriceps, hamstrings and calves. The alternating kick adds a dynamic component that improves balance and hip stability. It helps build upper body strength and lower leg power in a single fluid motion.
difficulty reason: Bodyweight exercise that uses basic push‑up mechanics with an added leg kick, requiring minimal equipment and coordination.

name: overhead triceps stretch
instructions:
Stand or sit upright with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Extend one arm overhead, bending at the elbow so that your hand reaches towards the opposite shoulder blade.
With your other hand, gently pull the elbow of the extended arm towards the opposite side of your head, feeling a stretch in your triceps.
Hold the stretch for 15-30 seconds, then release.
Repeat on the other side.
description: This overhead triceps stretch targets the triceps brachii and helps increase flexibility in the upper arms and shoulders, promoting better range of motion and reducing muscle tension.
difficulty reason: It requires only body weight and basic coordination, making it suitable for all fitness levels.

name: pelvic tilt
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands by your sides.
Engage your abs and tilt your pelvis upward, pressing your lower back into the ground.
Hold this position for a few seconds, focusing on contracting your abs.
Release the tilt and return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Pelvic tilts strengthen the lower abdominal and lumbar stabilizers by engaging the core while lying supine. This movement improves posture, reduces lower back strain, and enhances spinal mobility. It is a foundational exercise for core stability.
difficulty reason: It requires only bodyweight and minimal coordination, making it suitable for beginners.

name: pelvic tilt into bridge
instructions:
Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your arms by your sides with your palms facing down.
Engage your glutes and core muscles.
Tilt your pelvis upward, lifting your hips off the ground.
Hold the bridge position for a few seconds.
Slowly lower your hips back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the glutes, hamstrings, and core, helping to strengthen the posterior chain and improve spinal stability. It also enhances hip mobility and can aid in reducing lower back discomfort. Regular practice can lead to better posture and increased athletic performance.
difficulty reason: It is a simple bodyweight movement that requires minimal equipment and coordination.

name: peroneals stretch
instructions:
Sit on the ground with your legs extended in front of you.
Loop the rope around the ball of your foot and hold the ends of the rope with your hands.
Gently pull the rope towards you, flexing your foot and stretching your calf muscles.
Hold the stretch for 15-30 seconds.
Release the tension on the rope and repeat the stretch on the other leg.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This stretch targets the calf muscles and the surrounding ankle and foot musculature, helping to improve flexibility, reduce tightness, and enhance ankle mobility for better functional performance.
difficulty reason: It involves only a simple rope and basic body positioning, requiring minimal coordination and equipment.

name: pike-to-cobra push-up
instructions:
Start in a push-up position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and your feet together.
Engage your core and lift your hips up towards the ceiling, forming an inverted V shape with your body.
Lower your upper body towards the ground by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your body.
As you lower down, shift your weight forward and transition into a cobra pose by straightening your arms and lifting your chest up.
Reverse the movement by bending your elbows and lowering your chest back down towards the ground.
Push through your hands to return to the inverted V position.
Continue the movement by lowering your hips back down towards the ground, returning to the starting push-up position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The pike-to-cobra push-up targets the glutes, core, shoulders, and triceps, engaging the upper legs and lower body while enhancing overall stability and upper body strength. It improves shoulder mobility, core endurance, and hip flexibility through a dynamic transition between inverted V and cobra positions.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight compound exercise that requires coordination and moderate strength, fitting the intermediate level.

name: plyo push up
instructions:
Start in a high plank position with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Lower your chest towards the ground by bending your elbows, keeping your body in a straight line.
Push explosively off the ground, using your chest muscles to propel your upper body off the ground.
Land softly with your hands back in the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The plyo push‑up targets the pectorals, triceps, and shoulders while engaging the core for stability. It builds upper‑body power, improves explosive strength, and enhances muscular endurance. The explosive movement also boosts cardiovascular fitness and coordination.
difficulty reason: It requires body‑weight control and explosive coordination, fitting the intermediate level for those comfortable with standard push‑ups.

name: posterior step to overhead reach
instructions:
Stand with your feet hip-width apart and your arms by your sides.
Take a step back with your right foot, landing on the ball of your foot.
Bend your left knee and lower your body into a lunge position.
As you lower into the lunge, simultaneously reach your arms overhead.
Pause for a moment at the bottom of the lunge, then return to the starting position by pushing through your left heel and bringing your right foot forward.
Repeat the movement on the other side, stepping back with your left foot and bending your right knee.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This body‑weight move engages the core, glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps, and shoulders in a dynamic lunge while reaching overhead, improving stability, balance, and overall functional strength.
difficulty reason: It requires only body weight and basic coordination, making it accessible to those new to resistance training.

name: posterior tibialis stretch
instructions:
Sit on the ground with your legs extended in front of you.
Loop the rope around the ball of your foot and hold the ends of the rope with your hands.
Gently pull the rope towards you, flexing your foot and stretching your calf muscles.
Hold the stretch for 20-30 seconds.
Release the tension on the rope and relax your foot.
Repeat the stretch on the other leg.
description: This stretch targets the posterior tibialis and calf muscles, helping to improve ankle stability and reduce shin splints. It also gently lengthens the hamstrings and quadriceps for better lower‑leg flexibility. Regular practice can aid in recovery and enhance overall lower‑leg mobility.
difficulty reason: It uses only a rope and bodyweight, requiring minimal coordination and equipment.

name: potty squat
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward.
Lower your body down by bending your knees and pushing your hips back as if you were sitting on a chair.
Keep your chest up and your back straight throughout the movement.
Lower yourself until your thighs are parallel to the ground or as low as you can comfortably go.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push through your heels to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The potty squat works the quadriceps, glutes, and core while also engaging the lower back and hips for stability. It improves lower body strength, hip mobility, and core endurance, making it ideal for everyday functional fitness. By keeping the chest up and back straight, it also promotes good posture.
difficulty reason: It is a simple bodyweight squat that requires minimal equipment and coordination, making it accessible to beginners.

name: potty squat with support
instructions:
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward.
Hold onto a stable support, such as a chair or wall, for balance.
Lower your body down into a squat position by bending your knees and pushing your hips back.
Keep your chest up and your back straight throughout the movement.
Pause for a moment at the bottom, then push through your heels to return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This exercise targets the glutes, quadriceps, hamstrings, and calves, strengthening the lower body and enhancing hip stability. It improves balance and joint mobility while being low-impact and safe for beginners.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight squat with support, making it a simple movement that requires low coordination and is suitable for beginners.

name: power clean
instructions:
Start with the barbell on the ground in front of you, with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Bend down and grip the barbell with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your back straight and chest up as you lift the barbell off the ground, extending your hips and knees.
As the barbell reaches your mid-thigh, explosively pull it upwards, shrugging your shoulders and pulling your elbows high and to the sides.
As the barbell reaches its highest point, quickly drop underneath it, rotating your elbows around and catching the barbell on your shoulders in a front squat position.
Stand up with the barbell, fully extending your hips and knees.
Lower the barbell back down to the starting position, keeping control of the weight throughout the movement.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The power clean targets the hamstrings, glutes, quadriceps, and calves, engaging these muscles through a rapid hip- and knee-extension phase followed by a powerful pull. It improves explosive strength, power output, and full-body coordination, making it ideal for athletes and strength training.
difficulty reason: It requires coordination, timing, and the use of a barbell for a multi-joint explosive movement.

name: power point plank
instructions:
Start in a high plank position with your hands directly under your shoulders and your body in a straight line from head to toe.
Engage your core and squeeze your glutes to maintain a stable position.
Lower your body down onto your forearms, one arm at a time, maintaining a straight line from head to toe.
Hold this position for the desired amount of time, keeping your core and glutes engaged.
To return to the starting position, push through your forearms and lift your body back up into a high plank position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: This body‑weight plank variation targets the core, especially the abs, while also engaging the shoulders, triceps, and glutes for stability. It improves overall trunk strength, enhances posture, and boosts core endurance without requiring equipment. The movement also helps develop a strong, stable foundation for more advanced core exercises.
difficulty reason: The exercise uses only body weight with a straightforward plank motion, making it accessible for beginners with low coordination demands.

name: prisoner half sit-up (male)
instructions:
Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Place your hands behind your head with your elbows pointing outwards.
Engaging your abs, slowly lift your upper body off the ground, curling forward until your torso is at a 45-degree angle.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your upper body back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The prisoner half sit-up targets the core and hip flexors by lifting the torso while keeping the lower body stationary. It strengthens abdominal muscles and improves trunk stability. This bodyweight exercise can be performed anywhere without any equipment.
difficulty reason: It is a bodyweight core movement requiring minimal coordination and no equipment.

name: prone twist on stability ball
instructions:
Start by lying face down on a stability ball with your feet shoulder-width apart and your toes touching the ground.
Place your hands behind your head or cross them over your chest.
Engage your core muscles and slowly lift your upper body off the ball, keeping your back straight.
Rotate your torso to one side, bringing your shoulder towards your hip. Keep your hips and legs stable throughout the movement.
Pause for a moment, then return to the starting position.
Repeat the rotation to the other side.
Continue alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The prone twist on a stability ball engages the obliques and lower back, strengthening the core and improving rotational stability. It also helps enhance balance and posture by challenging the abdominal muscles in a dynamic, controlled manner.
difficulty reason: Because it uses only a stability ball and bodyweight, requiring minimal equipment and coordination.

name: pull up (neutral grip)
instructions:
Hang from a pull-up bar with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and your arms fully extended.
Engage your core and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Pull your body up towards the bar by bending your elbows and driving your elbows down towards your hips.
Continue pulling until your chin is above the bar.
Pause for a moment at the top, then slowly lower your body back down to the starting position with control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: The neutral-grip pull-up targets the latissimus dorsi, while also engaging the biceps and forearms, improving upper back width and grip strength. It enhances core stability and shoulder blade retraction for better posture and functional pulling power. Consistent practice builds strength, muscular endurance, and joint stability in the upper body.
difficulty reason: It is a compound bodyweight exercise requiring moderate upper body strength and coordination, fitting the intermediate level.

name: pull-in (on stability ball)
instructions:
Start by sitting on the stability ball with your feet flat on the ground and your knees bent at a 90-degree angle.
Place your hands on the sides of the stability ball for support.
Engage your abs and slowly roll your hips forward, bringing your knees towards your chest.
Pause for a moment at the top of the movement, then slowly extend your legs back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
description: Pull‑in on a stability ball engages the core and hip flexors by rolling the hips forward while seated, improving abdominal strength and stability.
difficulty reason: It’s a body‑weight exercise with minimal coordination and only a stability ball.

name: pull-up
instructions:
